1: Bildad the Shuhite is identified by region: Shuah was a son of Abraham by Keturah (Genesis 25:2), placing him in the Aramean/North Arabian belt. The three Friends represent the wisdom traditions of the eastern peoples — Eliphaz (Teman/Edomite), Bildad (Shuhite/Aramean), Zophar (Naamathite/unknown Arabian) — all respectable carriers of international wisdom tradition. Their credentials are part of the narrative setup: these are not ignorant accusers but representatives of the best human wisdom available.
2: Kammāh timallēl-rûaḥ — “how long will you speak wind?” The Hebrew rûaḥ (wind/spirit/breath) functions as a derogatory term for empty speech; Bildad is essentially calling Job’s lament hot air. The irony is that YHWH will speak from a storm/whirlwind (sᵉʿārāh) in chapter 38, and will condemn the Friends’ words, not Job’s, as theologically incorrect (Job 42:7).
3: Hăyᵉʿawwēl ʾēl mišpāṭ — “does God pervert justice?” The piel of ʿāwat (pervert/distort) uses the same root as ʿawlāh (wickedness, injustice). Bildad’s theology is correct in the abstract: YHWH does not pervert justice. The MKT renders “distort” to capture the spatial metaphor — justice bent, curved away from the straight. The error is not in Bildad’s theology but in his application: he assumes the present situation is a straightforward case of justice in operation.
4: ʾim-ḥāṭᵉʾû ḇānêḵā lô wayyᵉšallᵉḥēm biḏê pišʿām — “if your children sinned against him, he gave them over to the consequences of their own sin.” Pišʿām (their transgression) uses the same root as the legally weightiest word for deliberate rebellion; šillaḥ (gave them over) is the same word used for divine abandonment to consequences. Bildad is saying Job’s dead children got what their sins deserved — a brutal pastoral move against a grieving father, and one the narrative frame of chapters 1-2 has already refuted.
5: ʾim-ʾattāh tᵉšaḥēr ʾel-ʾēl — “if you will seek God earnestly.” Šāḥar means “to seek/look for early in the morning” — the dawn-seeking intensity of diligent prayer; the same root appears in Psalms 63:1 (“early I will seek you”). Ḥānan (plead for grace) is the grace-vocabulary of the Psalter; Bildad instructs Job to do exactly what Job has been doing — pleading before God — but frames it as the condition Job has not yet met.
6: ʾim-zak wᵉyāšār ʾāttāh kî-ʿattāh yāʿîr ʿāleḵā — “if you are pure and upright.” Zak (pure) and yāšār (upright) are the exact terms the narrator uses to describe Job in Job 1:1 (“blameless and upright” — tām wᵉyāšār); Bildad’s conditional uses Job’s own credentials as if they were yet to be established, while the reader knows they were established before the opening. The promise “he will restore your righteous home” will be fulfilled at the book’s end, but not because of Bildad’s advice.
7: wᵉhāyāh rēʾšîṯᵉḵā miṣʿār ûʾaḥărîṯᵉḵā yiśgeh mᵉʾōḏ — “though your beginnings were humble, your latter years will be far greater.” The saying echoes the conventional wisdom of restoration after humble beginnings; the identical promise is quoted in Job 42:12 when God actually does restore Job. Bildad gets the outcome right while completely misreading the path.
8: šāʾal-nāʾ lᵉḏôr rišôn — “ask the former generations.” The appeal to ancestral wisdom (ḏôr rišôn = first/former generation) is Bildad’s epistemological method: traditional wisdom accumulated across generations is the most reliable guide. This is contrasted with Job’s appeal to his own experience. The tension between inherited tradition and lived experience is one of the book’s structural concerns.
9: kî-ṯmôl ʾᵃnaḥnû wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ — “for we are but of yesterday and know nothing.” The phrase kî-ṯmôl (for yesterday/as of yesterday) is an idiom for temporal insignificance; compare the same expression in 1 Chronicles 29:15 (“our days on earth are like a shadow”) and Psalms 90:4 (a thousand years are like a watch in the night). Bildad’s epistemological humility about the present generation ironically applies more to himself than he realizes.
10: hălōʾ-hēmmāh yôrûḵā wᵉyōmrû lāḵ — “will they not teach you, tell you, and speak?” The three verbs of instruction (yārah = teach/instruct; ʾāmar = tell; ṣāʾ = bring forth words) describe the full transmission process of traditional wisdom. Yārah is the same root as tôrāh (instruction/Torah), placing Bildad’s appeal to tradition in the same category as divine instruction.
11: hăyiśgeh-gōmeʾ bilōʾ biṣṣāh — “can papyrus grow where there is no marsh?” Gōmeʾ (papyrus/bulrush) is an Egyptian loanword — the same plant Moses’ basket was made of (Exodus 2:3). The botanical analogy is precise: marsh plants require their specific habitat. The argument is analogical — just as these plants require their cause, suffering requires a cause (sin). The analogy is logically valid; the application to Job is not.
12: ʿōḏennû ḇᵉʾibbô lōʾ yiqqāṭēp wᵉlipnê ḵol-ḥāṣîr yiḇāš — “though it is still green and not yet cut, it withers before any other herb.” The sudden collapse of the plant that seemed well-watered is the analogy for the apparent prosperity of the wicked; yibbāš (wither) is the same verb used in Psalms 102:4 and Isaiah 40:7-8 (the grass withers). The reversal from green-and-lush to withered-before-anything-else captures the instability of prosperity without God.
13: kēn ʾorḥôṯ kol-šōkᵉḥê ʾēl wᵉtiqwaṯ ḥānēp tōʾḇēḏ — “such is the end of all who forget God; the hope of the godless will come to nothing.” Tiqwāh (hope) is a key Job-term, appearing also in Job 14:19 (you wash away the hope of man) and Job 19:10 (my hope he has uprooted like a tree). Bildad claims confident knowledge of whose hope will fail; the book’s irony is that it is Bildad’s own theological hope (Job is guilty and will be exposed) that fails.
14: ʾᵃšer-yāqôṭ kiślô wᵉbêṯ ʿakkāḇîš miṭḥô — “what he trusts in will be cut off, and his confidence is like a spider’s web.” ʿAkkāḇîš (spider) is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the OT; the image of spider-web fragility is striking precisely because the web appears intricate and strong while being wholly unable to bear weight. Kiślô (his confidence) shares a root with the constellation Kesil/Orion — both carry the sense of foolish overconfidence in something that will not hold.
15: yiššāʿēn ʿal-bêṯô wᵉlōʾ yaʿᵃmōḏ — “he leans against his house, but it does not stand.” The image of leaning on something that gives way is the standard OT figure for misplaced trust (cf. Ezekiel 29:7 — leaning on Egypt as a broken reed). The house (bêṯ) that cannot support the one who clings to it is both literal and metaphorical: the worldly establishment that prosperity-religion builds will not hold when tested.
16: rāṭōb hûʾ lipnê-šāmeš ûʿal gannāṯô yanṭᵉriḥa yônaqtô — “he is well-watered in the sunshine, his shoots spreading over the garden.” The image shifts from the spider’s web to a lush garden plant — the wicked man’s temporary prosperity is like rapid growth that looks impressive but is not rooted. The garden imagery inverts the Eden pattern: apparently flourishing, actually headed for destruction.
17: ʿal-gal šārāšāyw yᵉsûḇāḵû bêṯ ʾᵃḇānîm yehĕzeh — “his roots twine around the pile of stones; he forces his way into a stony place.” The aggressive root-growth into inhospitable stone looks like resilience but is actually rootlessness in disguise: there is no genuine nourishment in stone. The image of invasive roots finding purchase in rock only to be suddenly uprooted (Job 8:18) captures the sudden reversibility of ungodly prosperity.
18: ʾim-yᵉḇallᵉʿennû mimᵉqômô wᵉkiḥeš bô lōʾ rᵉʾîṯîḵā — “but if he is destroyed from his place, it says to him: ‘I have never seen you.’” The impersonal “it says” refers to the place itself — the land the wicked man inhabited denies knowing him. This personification of place as witness echoes Abel’s blood crying from the ground (Genesis 4:10); the earth itself testifies against the wicked.
19: hēn-hûʾ mᵉśôś darkô ûmeʿāpār ʾaḥēr yiṣmāḥû — “behold, such is the joy of his path; and from the earth others will spring up in his place.” The sarcastic “such is the joy” (mᵉśôś darkô) announces the replacement of the wicked by others — the earth continues indifferently. Bildad’s point is that the pattern recycles: the godless flourish and fall, and others take their place; only the righteous escape this cycle.
20: hēn ʾēl lōʾ yimʾas tām — “God does not reject the blameless.” Tām (blameless/complete) is the same word used of Job in 1:1 and Job 1:8; YHWH himself calls Job tām. Bildad’s assertion is true — God does not reject the blameless — and will be demonstrated by the book’s end. But Bildad’s implication (therefore Job is not blameless, since he is apparently rejected) is the error the narrative refutes.
21: ʿaḏ-yᵉmallēʾ śᵉḥôq pîḵā ûśpāṯeḵā tᵉrûʿāh — “he will yet fill your mouth with laughter and your lips with joyful shouts.” Śᵉḥôq (laughter) is the word used for Sarah’s laughter (Genesis 21:6) and for the laughter of restored Zion (Psalms 126:2). Bildad’s promise of restored laughter is ironically accurate: in Job 42:10-17, Job’s latter days are greater; but the path there is through the whirlwind, not through Bildad’s prescribed repentance.
22: śōnᵉʾêḵā yilbᵉšû-ḇōšeṯ wᵉʾōhel rᵉšāʿîm ʾênennû — “your enemies will be clothed with shame, and the tent of the wicked will vanish.” The reversal-of-garments motif — enemies wearing shame while the righteous wear joy (cf. Psalms 109:29) — is standard covenant-theology language. The “tent of the wicked” that vanishes echoes Job 8:15’s house that cannot stand. Bildad ends with standard encouragement, theologically correct, pastorally catastrophic in its implicit accusation.
